text: Community detection is a widely used technique in networks analysis. In this paper, we give an overview of the state of the art of overlapping community detection, focusing on algorithms, measures and benchmarks. We made a comparison between 14 algorithms. The last part is dedicated to the evaluation of the precision of an algorithm in detecting overlapping nodes, which corresponds to solving the overdetection and underdetection problems. To this end, we propose a new framework called FOC combining community level evaluation metrics such as normalized mutual information and Omega index with node level evaluation measures such as the F-score. This allows us to determine that, for low overlapping density networks, the best algorithms are SLPA, OSLOM, Game and COPRA. For high overlapping density networks and diversity, SLPA and Game are the most stable algorithms, but the resolution of the community structures in such cases is still an open problem. Finally, we evaluate the overlapping structure of real world graphs, and we show that they all have the same common characteristic: the overlapping nodes are always limited, i.e. less than 30% of the nodes, and the number of communities to which they belong is always limited, i.e. less than 3 or 4.
